naming_inspiration.description: Named 'Moses Porter' for grandfather Col. Moses Porter (HD2434).

institutions.institution_blurb: Licensed to preach in 1868 at Talcott Street Congregational Church (now Faith Congregational Church) — historic Black Congregational congregation founded 1826 in Hartford.

military_service.rank: Private (1862-64) → First Lieutenant and Brevet Captain (1864)
units: 39th United States Colored Troops (1864) — Black Union Army regiment
Aide-de-Camp to General Duncan, New Bern North Carolina (1865)
military_service.role: Enlisted Massachusetts state regiment 1862; transitioned to officer commission with one of the U.S. Colored Troops Black Union regiments in 1864. Served as aide-de-camp to General Duncan in New Bern North Carolina through war's end.

burial.plot_notes: Congressional Cemetery, Washington, District of Columbia. Cemetery linkage repair: existing person was listed in cemetery hooker_connections in prior pilots but burial.cemetery_id was null. Pilot104 closure of bidirectional reference.

narrative_blocks.body: Moses Porter Snell rose from Private in the 36th Massachusetts Volunteers (1862-1864) to First Lieutenant and Brevet Captain in the 39th United States Colored Troops in 1864 — one of the Black Union Army regiments. He served as Aide-de-Camp to General Duncan in New Bern North Carolina in 1865. Immediately postwar he became Superintendent of the Freedmen's Bureau in Washington D.C., a significant Reconstruction-era role coordinating federal support for newly emancipated African Americans.

narrative_blocks.header: Licensed to preach at Talcott Street Church, 1868
narrative_blocks.body: After ordination on May 8, 1867 from Hartford Theological Seminary, Moses Porter Snell was licensed to preach in 1868 at Talcott Street Congregational Church in Hartford — the historic Black Congregational congregation that had been founded in 1826. The Talcott Street licensing parallel between his Reconstruction-era career and his ministerial commissioning at one of New England's most prominent Black churches is striking. He spent the next forty years as missionary and pastor in Washington D.C. and Maryland-Virginia churches.

narrative_blocks.header: Grandson of Amherst College founder Thomas Snell
narrative_blocks.body: Moses Porter Snell was grandson of Dr. Thomas Snell, founder of Amherst College, and nephew of Ebenezer S. Snell (Amherst class of 1822). He himself graduated Amherst in 1868 and attended Hartford Theological Seminary 1866-1868. The two-generation Amherst College connection — founder grandfather, alumnus uncle, alumnus himself — was characteristic of the New England Congregational scholarly families that dominated late-nineteenth-century mainline Protestant institutional leadership.

narrative_blocks.header: Born North Brookfield MA, buried Congressional Cemetery DC
narrative_blocks.body: Moses Porter Snell was born May 3, 1839 in North Brookfield Massachusetts and died December 31, 1909 in Newton New Jersey at age seventy. He was buried at Congressional Cemetery in Washington D.C. He married Mary Cordelia Hallock on August 31, 1862 — the same year he enlisted in the 36th Massachusetts. They had nine children.

parents.mother_research_notes: Mother unknown. Cannot be Minnie Gilbert Andrews (HD2560's known wife) who died 1909 — Priscilla was born 1913. Either second wife not yet documented or other source. CHRONOLOGY FLAG.

narrative_blocks.header: Dominican Sister at Adrian, Michigan motherhouse
narrative_blocks.body: Priscilla Edwards Snell entered the Adrian Dominican Sisters in Michigan — one of the major American Roman Catholic congregations of Dominican women religious founded in 1879. She is buried at the Adrian Dominican Sisters Cemetery at the Adrian motherhouse. The Catholic vocation marks a denominational departure from the family's Snell-side Congregational and Baptist roots in mid-nineteenth-century North Brookfield Massachusetts.

narrative_blocks.header: Mother unknown; chronology rules out documented stepmother
narrative_blocks.body: Priscilla was the daughter of Merwin-Marie Fitz Porter Snell — but her mother is not yet documented. Merwin's known wife Minnie Gilbert Andrews Snell died in 1909, four years before Priscilla's 1913 birth in Old Greenwich Connecticut; either Merwin remarried between 1910 and 1913 (second wife not yet surfacing in sources) or Priscilla was born outside marriage. The chronology question is open pending further research.

burial.plot_notes: Congressional Cemetery, Washington, District of Columbia. Cemetery linkage repair: existing person was listed in cemetery hooker_connections in prior pilots but burial.cemetery_id was null. Pilot104 closure of bidirectional reference.

military_service.rank: U.S. Army officer; entered as junior officer
military_service.role: Served as United States Army officer during the 1898 Spanish-American War and stayed in service until 1911. Likely served at multiple campaigns of the era given the length of service.
Returned to U.S. Army for World War I 1917-1918; attained the rank of Major (Infantry) before his 1919 discharge. Whether he accompanied the American Expeditionary Force to France is unknown.

narrative_blocks.header: Spanish-American War to WWI Major Infantry
narrative_blocks.body: Charles Livingston Snell served as a United States Army officer during the 1898 Spanish-American War and remained in service until 1911 — a thirteen-year active-duty span across the short campaigns of that era. He then returned to the Army for World War I from 1917 to 1918, attaining the rank of Major (Infantry) before his 1919 discharge. Whether he accompanied the American Expeditionary Force to France during the war is unknown.

narrative_blocks.header: Navy fingerprint expert, USMC and federal clerk
narrative_blocks.body: Charles Livingston Snell's postwar federal career — per census records — included service as a United States Navy fingerprint expert, then as a United States Marine Corps clerk, then as a United States Government chief clerk. The career arc combined three different federal agencies and reflected the kind of long-term Civil Service work that flourished in early-twentieth-century Washington.

narrative_blocks.header: Wife Sarah died 1918, possibly Spanish Flu
narrative_blocks.body: Charles Livingston Snell married Sarah Elizabeth Rittenhouse (1880-1918) on May 16, 1906 in Washington D.C., and they had three children — including Elizabeth Rittenhouse Snell Hayes who married Lieutenant General Charles Harold Hayes. Sarah died at age thirty-seven on October 17, 1918, possibly a victim of the Spanish flu pandemic of 1918-1920 (unsourced). Charles never remarried and died at age eighty-three on April 19, 1961 at the Fort Ward Convalescent Home in Alexandria Virginia, buried at Arlington National Cemetery with Sarah.

narrative_blocks.header: Married future LTG Charles Harold Hayes USMC
narrative_blocks.body: Elizabeth Rittenhouse Snell married Charles Harold Hayes, who would rise to become the 9th Assistant Commandant of the United States Marine Corps and retire as Lieutenant General. At the time of their marriage Hayes was a junior Naval Academy graduate; by his retirement decades later he had commanded multiple Marine Aircraft Wings and the Marine Corps Air Station Kaneohe Bay. She did not live to see most of his career; she died young.

Elizabeth Rittenhouse Snell Hayes died September 3, 1940 in San Diego California at age thirty-three. She is buried at Arlington National Cemetery — where her father Major Charles Livingston Snell, mother Sarah Elizabeth Rittenhouse Snell, and eventually her husband Lt. Gen. Charles Harold Hayes (d. 1995) would all also be buried.

burialCemetery.name: Congressional Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Historic cemetery established by Christ Church Washington Parish. Contains graves of numerous members of Congress, J. Edgar Hoover, and John Philip Sousa.

INST091.primary_name: Talcott Street Congregational Church (now Faith Congregational Church)
INST091.name: Talcott Street Congregational Church (now Faith Congregational Church)
INST091.notes:
Founded 1826 by African Americans in Hartford after segregated treatment in white churches. Originally housed at Talcott Street, the congregation later took the name Faith Congregational Church. The church operated a school for African American children in its lower level — Hartford's regular school system technically accepted all children but African American parents established separate schools (1830) due to discriminatory treatment. The school met in the church's two lower rooms.

Rev. James W.C. Pennington pastored the congregation from 1840 to 1847 — writing during this period the first published history of African Americans (1841) and serving as a delegate to the Connecticut Anti-Slavery Society. It was here that John Hooker (H01626) became Pennington's friend and later, in 1851, legally purchased Pennington's freedom from his Maryland enslaver after the 1850 Fugitive Slave Law endangered him.

Other notable members of the period: Deacon James Mars (Connecticut Anti-Slavery Society executive committee); Henry Foster (state temperance society organizer); Ann Plato (teacher and author); Henry and Tamar Wilson (missionaries who accompanied the Mende return to Sierra Leone after the Amistad ruling).

INST018.name: Amherst College
INST018.notes: Founded 1821. One of the oldest liberal arts colleges in the United States. Merrill Edwards Gates (HD1687) served as 6th President 1890-1899. Wikipedia: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Amherst_College David Peck Todd (HD1758) was astronomy professor and observatory director 1881-1917. Millicent Todd Bingham (HD1759) received honorary LL.D. 1957.
